dataElements.name: Heatwave-data-collection-point-geo-codes
dataElements.shortName: Heatwave-data-collection-point-geo-codes
dataElements.description: Please collect GPS coordinates at the point of data collection. Ensure that the device's location services are enabled, and that you are physically present at the collection site before submitting.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.1 Is the Health Workforce Equipped with a plan to identify and protect health workers at risk of heat stress?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.1 Is the Health Workforce Equip
dataElements.description: 1: No plan:No plan or measures in place to identify or protect health workers from heat stress risks. 2: Minimal plan:Basic awareness exists, but no formal plan; limited or informal measures may be taken reactively. 3: Basic plan:A draft or informal plan exists with some measures implemented, but it's not comprehensive or routinely applied. 4: Moderate plan:A formal, documented plan is in place and regularly implemented; includes protocols for identifying and protecting at-risk health workers. 5: Fully developed plan:A comprehensive, regularly updated plan is in place and fully operational; includes training, monitoring, early warning systems, and active mitigation measures for protecting health workers from heat stress.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.2 Is the Health Workforce Provided with appropriate clothes (e.g. light, loose-fitting cotton clothes, and when necessary, a hat)?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.2 Is the Health Workforce Provi
dataElements.description: 1: No guidance in place:No clothing guidance or provision is available. Staff wear regular uniforms or attire not suited to heat conditions. 2: Minimal guidance in place:Staff may receive informal advice on suitable clothing, but no appropriate clothes or hats are supplied by the facility. 3: Basic clothing in place:Some appropriate clothing or hats are provided, but only to specific staff or on an irregular basis. No consistent system in place. 4: Moderately appropriate clothing in place:Most staff are provided with appropriate, heat-adapted clothing and hats when needed. Some gaps in availability or consistency may still exist. 5: Fully appropriate clothing in place:All relevant staff are consistently and proactively provided with suitable clothing and hats designed for heat protection, as part of a formal occupational safety protocol.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.3 Is the Health Workforce Provided with sunscreen, hat and plenty of drinking water for staff carrying out outdoor activities?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.3 Is the Health Workforce Provi
dataElements.description: 1: No Preparation:No sunscreen, hats, or drinking water are provided for staff working outdoors. Staff rely entirely on personal arrangements. 2: Minimal Preparation:Drinking water may be informally available, but no structured provision of sunscreen or hats. Measures are ad hoc and not tailored to outdoor work conditions. 3: Basic Preparation:Some protective items (e.g., hats or water) are provided for certain staff or during specific activities. Sunscreen may be occasionally available. Distribution is inconsistent and not standardized. 4: Moderate Preparation:Most outdoor staff are provided with hats, sunscreen, and drinking water. Provision is generally reliable but may not be fully systematic or universal. 5: Full Preparation:All staff engaged in outdoor activities are consistently provided with sunscreen, hats, and adequate drinking water as part of a structured occupational health and safety protocol.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.4 Is the Health Workforce Provided with safe water during a heatwave event and stimulated regularly for appropriate water intake
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.4 Is the Health Workforce Provi
dataElements.description: 1: Not provided:No provision of safe drinking water is made during heatwave events. Staff are not reminded or encouraged to stay hydrated. 2: Minimally provided Drinking water may be informally available, but it is not consistently safe or readily accessible during heatwaves:No structured reminders or hydration prompts are in place. 3: Basically provided:Safe drinking water is available to some staff or in select areas, and occasional reminders to drink water are given, but efforts are inconsistent and not part of a formal system. 4: Moderately Provided:Safe and accessible drinking water is available throughout the facility during heatwaves, and most staff receive regular reminders to stay hydrated, though coverage may still have minor gaps. 5: Fully Provided:Safe and accessible drinking water is available throughout the facility during heatwaves, and most staff receive regular reminders to stay hydrated, though coverage may still have minor gaps.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.5 Is the Health Workforce Provided with a cool space or a shower room for staff
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.5 Is the Health Workforce Provi
dataElements.description: 1: Not Provided:No designated cool space or shower room is available for staff, even during extreme heat conditions. 2: Minimally Provided:There may be informal or shared areas with limited ventilation that staff occasionally use to cool down, but no dedicated or equipped cool space or shower room is provided. 3: Basically Provided:A cool space or shower room exists but is only accessible to a limited number of staff, may not be well-maintained, or is functional only during certain hours or conditions. 4: Moderately Provided:Most staff have access to a designated cool space or shower room that is functional and reasonably maintained. However, some issues with access, capacity, or upkeep may still exist. 5: Fully Provided:All staff have consistent and unrestricted access to a well-equipped, clean, and functional cool space or shower room designed to provide relief from heat, as part of an established occupational safety and wellness policy.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.6 Is the Health Workforce Provided with an information system to manage occupational safety and health in the facility during a heatwave, including rest for staff
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-hr-1.1.6 Is the Health Workforce Provi
dataElements.description: 1: Not Provided:"No information system or structured approach exists to manage occupational safety and health during heatwaves. Staff receive no guidance or support related to rest or heat-related risks. 2: Minimally Provided; Basic, informal communication may occur (e:g., verbal reminders), but there is no formal system or documentation to manage occupational safety, health, or rest schedules during heatwaves. 3: Basically Provided:An information system or basic framework exists but is limited in scope. It may include partial staff coverage, outdated information, or lack protocols for structured rest or protection measures during heatwaves. 4: Moderately Provided:A functional information system is in place to manage occupational health and safety during heatwaves, including guidance on rest. However, implementation may be uneven across departments or lacking in regular updates. 5: Fully Provided:A comprehensive, up-to-date information system is fully operational, actively guiding and monitoring occupational safety and health during heatwaves. It includes protocols for staff rest, hydration, exposure limits, and clear lines of communication across all levels.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.1 Is the Health Workforce Trained on public health and climate change hazards, including health impacts related to heatwaves?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.1 Is the Health Workforce Train
dataElements.description: 1: No Training:No training has been provided on public health and climate change hazards. Staff are unaware of the health impacts of heatwaves. 2: Minimal Training:Only a few staff have received informal or general awareness on climate-related health issues. Heatwave-specific impacts are not adequately covered. 3: Basic Training:Some staff have received basic formal training on public health and climate change, with limited but relevant content on heatwave-related health impacts. Training is not standardized or widely implemented. 4: Moderate Training:Most staff have undergone structured training that includes climate change hazards and the health impacts of heatwaves. Some gaps may exist in refresher training or practical application. 5: Full/Comprehensive Training:All relevant staff have received comprehensive, up-to-date training on public health and climate change hazards, including in-depth coverage of heatwave-related health impacts. Training is standardized, routinely updated, and integrated into institutional capacity-building programs.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.2 Is the Health Workforce Trained to manage hazardous waste (chemical, biological, radiological)?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.2 Is the Health Workforce Train
dataElements.description: 1: No Training:No staff have received training on hazardous waste management. There is no awareness of the proper handling or disposal of chemical, biological, or radiological waste. 2: Minimal Training:A few staff have informal or outdated knowledge on hazardous waste management. Training is not standardized and does not cover all hazard types (chemical, biological, radiological). 3: Basic Training:Some staff have received basic training on hazardous waste management, covering at least one or two types of hazardous waste. However, the training may not be comprehensive or facility-wide. 4: Moderate Contingency Plan:The facility has a well-developed contingency plan, and most staff are aware of its content and trained on its implementation. Simulations or periodic reviews may take place, though gaps in full operationalization may still exist. 5: Full/Comprehensive Contingency Plan:A comprehensive, up-to-date contingency plan is fully integrated into facility operations. All relevant staff are trained and capable of following through with timely and effective actions to mitigate health workforce risks from heat stress, water- and air-borne diseases, and cardiovascular or respiratory conditions. Regular drills and reviews ensure continuous readiness.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.3 Is the Health Workforce Prepared and able to follow-up a contingency plan for emerging health workforce heat stress, water- and air-borne diseases, and cardiovascular and respiratory problems?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.3 Is the Health Workforce Prepa
dataElements.description: 1: No Contingency Plan:No contingency plan exists, and staff are unprepared to respond to health risks such as heat stress or climate-sensitive diseases affecting the health workforce. 2: Minimal Contingency Plan:A draft or informal plan may exist, but staff are largely unaware of it and have not been trained or oriented on how to implement it in practice. 3: Basic Contingency Plan:A contingency plan is in place and has been shared with some staff. Basic preparedness activities or limited drills may have occurred, but coverage or capacity remains inconsistent. 4: Moderate Active Engagement:Actively participates in the development of flood risk plans and responses, contributing to key decisions and processes, though not leading the efforts. 5: Full Engagement:Fully engaged in the development of flood risk plans and responses, taking a leadership role in shaping strategies and coordinating efforts.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.4 Is the Health Workforce Able to implement a contingency plan for public health emergencies, in case of high temperature effects, and water and food contamination?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.4 Is the Health Workforce Able
dataElements.description: 1: No Preparation to Implement Plan:No contingency plan exists, and the facility is unprepared to respond to public health emergencies related to high temperatures or water and food contamination. 2: Minimall Preparation to Implement Plan:A contingency plan may exist in draft or outdated form, but implementation capacity is weak. Staff are largely unaware of the plan or their roles in an emergency. 3: Basic Preparation:A basic contingency plan is available and shared with some staff. The facility can implement limited response actions during emergencies but lacks full coordination, resources, or practice. 4: Moderate Preparation:A structured and relatively up-to-date contingency plan is in place. Most staff are trained and equipped to implement it in case of high temperature effects or contamination incidents. Simulations or prior activations may have occurred, but some operational gaps remain. 5: Full Preparation:The facility has a comprehensive, practiced, and routinely updated contingency plan. All relevant staff are well-trained and able to effectively implement emergency measures in response to high temperatures, water, or food contamination. The plan is integrated into broader emergency preparedness systems with clear communication and coordination mechanisms.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.5 Is the Health Workforce Trained and have specific and clear guidance on actions to reduce heat risk factors for staff?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.5 Is the Health Workforce Train
dataElements.description: 1: No Training/Guidance:No training or guidance is provided. Staff are unaware of heat-related risks or appropriate preventive measures. 2: Minimal Training/Guidance:Staff have received minimal or informal training. Guidance, if any, is generic and not tailored to the facility or specific heat-related risks. 3: Basic Training/Guidance:Some staff have received basic training, and there is limited or non-comprehensive guidance available. Actions to reduce heat risks are known but not consistently implemented. 4: Moderate Training/Guidance in use:Most staff are trained, and there is clear, facility-specific guidance on actions to reduce heat risk factors. Guidance is generally followed, though refresher training or updates may be needed. 5: Full Training/Guidance:All relevant staff are fully trained, and there is specific, practical, and regularly updated guidance in place. Heat risk reduction measures are actively implemented, monitored, and integrated into routine facility operations and occupational health protocols.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.6 Is the Health Workforce Aware of the need for a plan to adjust outdoor health workers’ schedules—limiting work to cooler hours or using frequent rest breaks in shaded or cool areas during peak heat.
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-cd-1.2.6 Is the Health Workforce Aware
dataElements.description: 1: No Awareness:No awareness exists regarding the need for adjusting work schedules or rest periods for outdoor staff during extreme heat. No discussions or considerations have been made. 2: Minimal Awareness:Some awareness exists among a few staff or managers, but there is no structured plan or policy in place. Adjustments, if any, are ad hoc and not based on heat risk considerations. 3: Basic Awareness:The facility recognizes the need for an alternative action plan and has informal practices in place to adjust work during hot periods. However, these are inconsistently applied and not institutionalized. 4: Moderate Plan:Most relevant staff are aware, and a structured plan is in place to adjust outdoor work schedules and rest cycles during heatwaves. The plan is used but may not be routinely updated or universally enforced. 5: Full Awareness:Full awareness exists at all levels, and a well-defined, evidence-based action plan is implemented and routinely updated. Outdoor staff work on adjusted schedules with planned rest periods in cooler areas, supported by policy and actively monitored for effectiveness.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-car-1.3.1 Is the Health Workforce Aware about impacts of hot temperatures on human health via water quality and quantity (including water- and food-borne diseases) and air quality?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-car-1.3.1 Is the Health Workforce Awar
dataElements.description: 1: No Awareness:No awareness exists among staff about the link between high temperatures and their impact on water quality/quantity, air quality, or related health outcomes. 2: Minimal Awareness:Some staff have basic or informal understanding of heat-related impacts on water and air quality, but knowledge is fragmented and not grounded in public health guidance or training. 3: Basic Awareness:Staff have some structured awareness through basic training or orientation, and they understand the general connection between heat and health risks like water-/food-borne diseases and air pollution. However, understanding is limited in scope or depth. 4: Moderate Awareness:Most staff are aware and have received formal training or communication materials highlighting the specific health impacts of high temperatures via water and air systems. Information is applied in some areas of work. 5: Full Awareness:All relevant staff have comprehensive awareness and understanding of the impacts of heat on water quality, quantity, and air quality, and their links to human health. Knowledge is based on up-to-date evidence, reinforced through ongoing training, and integrated into operational and risk management planning.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-car-1.3.2 Is the Health Workforce Aware of the type of patients and symptoms expected during a heatwave?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-car-1.3.2 Is the Health Workforce Awar
dataElements.description: 1: No Awareness:No awareness of the health impacts of heatwaves or the types of patients/symptoms likely to present. Staff are unprepared to recognize or respond to heat-related illnesses. 2: Minimal Awareness:Some staff have a general awareness that heatwaves can affect health but lack specific knowledge of patient risk groups or common symptoms such as heat exhaustion, dehydration, or heat stroke. 3: Basic Awareness:Staff are somewhat aware of heat-related health risks and can identify a few vulnerable groups (e.g. elderly, children) and basic symptoms, but understanding is incomplete or not uniformly shared among staff. 4: Moderate Awareness:Most relevant staff are aware of the patient categories at higher risk (e.g. those with cardiovascular or respiratory conditions) and can recognize key heatwave-related symptoms. This knowledge is based on formal training or guidelines. 5: Full Awareness:All staff involved in patient care are fully trained and knowledgeable about the types of patients likely to be affected during a heatwave, the full spectrum of symptoms, and how to respond. This awareness is reinforced by regular training, clinical protocols, and integration into emergency preparedness planning.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-car-1.3.3 Is the Health Workforce Informed on how to use and follow a surveillance system to track health outcomes?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-car-1.3.3 Is the Health Workforce Info
dataElements.description: 1: No Awareness:No awareness of the importance of hydration or appropriate clothing in preventing heat-related health issues. 2: Minimal Awareness:Basic awareness exists, but staff have not received formal training. Knowledge is superficial, and staff are unable to effectively use or interpret the system. 3: Basic Awareness:Some staff have received introductory training and can perform limited functions within the system. Use is irregular, and there may be uncertainty around interpreting the data or taking appropriate follow-up actions. 4: Moderate Awareness:Most relevant staff are adequately trained and informed. They can competently use the surveillance system to enter, monitor, and report health data, with moderate guidance. 5: Full Awareness:All designated staff are thoroughly trained and fully informed on how to operate and follow the surveillance system. They can accurately track, analyze, and respond to health outcomes independently, and the system is well-integrated into routine public health monitoring and decision-making.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-car-1.3.4 Is the Health Workforce Aware of the need to keep hydrated and wear appropriate clothing?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-car-1.3.4 Is the Health Workforce Awar
dataElements.description: 1: No Awareness:No awareness of the importance of hydration or appropriate clothing in preventing heat-related health issues. 2: Minimal Awareness:Some awareness exists, but it is informal or inconsistent. Staff may not fully understand why hydration and suitable clothing matter during hot weather 3: Basic Awareness:Basic understanding among most staff about the need to stay hydrated and wear light, loose-fitting clothing. However, this may not be consistently practiced or reinforced through guidance. 4: Moderate Awareness:Strong awareness among staff. The importance of hydration and appropriate clothing is well understood and generally followed, with occasional reminders or educational materials provided. 5: Full Awareness:Comprehensive and consistent awareness across all staff. Hydration and appropriate clothing are emphasized as part of workplace health and safety protocols, supported by regular training, signage, and management practices.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-car-1.3.5 Is the Health Workforce Provided with a community health educational programme to improve community health in the face of heatwave risks?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-car-1.3.5 Is the Health Workforce Prov
dataElements.description: 1: Not Provided:No community health education programme exists related to heatwave risks. No materials, sessions, or outreach efforts are in place. 2: Minimally Provided:Some informal or ad hoc efforts (e.g., verbal advice or posters) exist, but there is no structured programme or consistent outreach. Limited reach and low impact. 3: Basically Provided:A basic community health education programme is in place, reaching some sections of the population. It may include sporadic sessions, materials, or campaigns, but lacks wide coverage or regularity. 4: Moderately Providded:A structured and ongoing educational programme is available and implemented in most areas. It includes regular sessions, materials, and guidance focused on community resilience to heatwave risks. 5: Fully/Comprehensively provided:A comprehensive, well-funded, and community-wide health education programme is actively implemented. It effectively informs and empowers the public on preventive measures, symptoms of heat-related illnesses, and available services during heatwaves, with continuous monitoring and feedback loops to improve effectiveness.

dataElements.name: heatwave-hw-car- 1.3.6 Is the Health Workforce Aware of ways to keep the facility cool - e.g., manage windows, curtains, lights, and use fans or cooler rooms - especially when temperatures are below 35°C.
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-hw-car- 1.3.6 Is the Health Workforce Awa
dataElements.description: 1: No Awareness:No awareness among staff regarding the importance of managing indoor temperatures or methods to keep the facility environment cool during heatwaves. 2: Minimal Awareness:Basic awareness exists among a few staff, but recommended actions (e.g. managing windows, curtains, and equipment use) are not consistently known or applied. 3: Basic Awareness:Basic awareness exists among a few staff, but recommended actions (e.g. managing windows, curtains, and equipment use) are not consistently known or applied. 4: Moderate Awareness:Most staff are aware and follow recommended practices to keep the facility cool. Cooling measures are regularly applied, though some may not be fully optimized or reinforced with guidance. 5: Full Awareness:All staff are fully aware and trained on maintaining a cool facility environment. Best practices are consistently implemented, monitored, and supported by internal guidelines or protocols. The facility environment is proactively managed during heat events.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.1.1. Does the health care facility verify water safety conditions, including updated risk assessments to map water resources and water supplies for the facility?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.1.1. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:The healthcare facility does not verify water safety conditions and has no assessment plan to map water resources or supplies. 2: Minimal Plan:The healthcare facility has begun initial discussions on verifying water safety and mapping water sources, but no formal plan exists. 3: Basic Plan:A draft risk assessment plan to map water resources and assess water safety is available but not yet finalised or implemented. 4: Moderate Plan in Place:The facility has an existing plan to verify water safety and map water resources, but it has not been updated to reflect current risks. 5: Fully Updated Plan:The healthcare facility has a comprehensive, regularly updated risk assessment plan that verifies water safety and maps all water sources and supplies.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.1.2 Does the health care facility have an evaluation system to monitor its water system or supply before, during and after the event?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.1.2 Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Evaluation System:The facility does not have any form of evaluation system to monitor the water system during emergency phases. 2: Minimal System in Consideration:Initial conversations or informal measures are in place, but no structured system exists to evaluate water supply during emergencies. 3: Basic System Developed:A draft or partial evaluation system exists, but it only addresses one or two of the event phases (before, during, or after). 4: Moderate Evaluation System in Place:A structured evaluation system covers most event phases but may lack consistency, full documentation, or regular updates. 5: Full/ Comprehensive Evaluation System Operational:A fully developed and consistently used system monitors the water system before, during, and after events. It is regularly tested and updated.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.1.3 Does the health care facility Have information on the water system installation that ensures lower risk of being contaminated?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.1.3 Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Information Available:The facility does not have records or design details of its water system that relate to contamination risk. 2: Minimal Documentation:Some fragmented or outdated installation details exist, with limited relevance to contamination risks. 3: Basic information Exist:Basic documentation of installation is available and includes general safety considerations but lacks technical depth. 4: Moderate Information in Use:Detailed information on water system installation is available and somewhat used to minimise contamination risk, though not comprehensively 5: Full information exist:Comprehensive and up-to-date documentation exists and is actively used to manage and reduce contamination risks in the facility's water system.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.1.4 Does the health care facility have a quality monitoring plan for water meant for human consumption?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.1.4 Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Monitoring Plan:The facility does not have a quality monitoring plan for water intended for human consumption. 2: Minimal Plan:Occasional, informal checks may occur, but no structured monitoring plan is in place. 3: Basic Plan:A basic plan for quality monitoring exists but is not consistently implemented or maintained. 4: Moderate Plan Implemented:A structured monitoring plan is operational, covering key aspects of water quality but may lack periodic review. 5: Full/Comprehensive and Updated Plan:A fully developed plan exists with clearly defined procedures for monitoring potable water. It is regularly reviewed and compliant with safety standards.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.1.5 Does the health care facility Have a monitoring plan for potable water?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.1.5 Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:The facility does not have any monitoring system for potable water. 2: Minimal Plan:Some informal practices may exist, but they are inconsistent and undocumented. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:A basic draft plan exists but is not widely implemented or updated. 4: Moderate Plan Active:A monitoring plan is implemented with partial coverage or outdated standards. 5: Fully Functional Plan:A comprehensive and up-to-date potable water monitoring plan is in place and actively used.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.1. Does the health care facility have a water management plan to identify water contamination?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.1. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:The facility does not have any water management strategy or framework to identify contamination. 2: Minimal Plan:There are early-stage discussions or considerations but no documented or active plan. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:a draft water management plan is available, focusing on identifying contamination but lacking implementation or regular review. 4: Moderate Plan in Place:The plan is operational and includes protocols to identify contamination risks, though it may not be comprehensive or updated regularly. 5: Fully Implemented Plan:A complete, regularly reviewed, and operational water management plan is in place to effectively identify and respond to contamination.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.2. Does the health care facility have onsite water purification equipment to provide safe drinking water?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.2. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Equipment Available:The facility does not have any onsite purification system or process for providing safe drinking water. 2: Minimal Provisions in Place:Limited or outdated equipment is available but not consistently used or maintained. 3: Basic Purification System Installed:Basic equipment is installed but may only cover part of the facility or lacks regular servicing. 4: Moderate Equipment Coverage:The facility has operational purification equipment that serves key areas but may need upgrades or expanded reach. 5: Fully Functional and Maintained:Equipment	Modern purification equipment is fully installed, well-maintained, and ensures safe drinking water throughout the facility.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.3 Does the health care facility provide sufficient drinking-water to staff, patients and visitors?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.3 Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Provision Made:The facility does not ensure access to drinking-water for any of the groups mentioned. 2: Minimal Access Provided:Drinking-water is available to some, but not all staff, patients, or visitors, and is inconsistently supplied. 3: Basic Supply Established:A basic provision exists for all groups, but may not meet full demand or frequency. 4: Moderate Coverage Available:Drinking-water is generally sufficient, though occasional shortages or unequal distribution may occur. 5: Fully Sufficient and Regular Supply:Drinking-water is adequately and consistently provided to staff, patients, and visitors at all times.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.4. Does the health care facility keep drinking water cool or refrigerated where possible for staff, patients and visitors?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.4. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Cooling Provided:Drinking water is not cooled or refrigerated, regardless of climate or facility conditions. 2: Minimal Cooling Efforts:Occasional or ad-hoc refrigeration is provided in limited areas but not systematically. 3: Basic Cooling Available:Cooling or refrigeration is available but limited in coverage or reliability. 4: Moderate System in Place:A reasonably consistent cooling/refrigeration setup exists in most areas of the facility. 5: Fully Functional Cooling System:Drinking water is kept cool or refrigerated across the facility with reliable, well-maintained systems.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.5. Does the health care facility have a contingency plan for monitoring and reducing contaminant concentrations in the facility water system supplies?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.5. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Contingency Plan:The facility does not have any emergency or contingency measures to monitor or reduce contaminants in water. 2: Minimal Planning Discussed:Some awareness or discussions have occurred, but no formal contingency measures are documented. 3: Basic Draft Exists:A draft contingency plan is available but not implemented or routinely reviewed. 4: Moderate Plan Activated:A functional plan exists and is occasionally activated, but may need further development or regular testing. 5: Fully Integrated and Reviewed Plan:A robust, tested, and regularly reviewed contingency plan exists to monitor and reduce contaminants in the water supply.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.6.Does the health care facility have water storage protected from direct sunlight?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.6.Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Protection in Place:Water storage is fully exposed to direct sunlight, increasing the risk of temperature-related degradation. 2: Minimal Protection Provided:Some improvised shade or covering exists but is inadequate for full protection. 3: Basic Protection Measures:Basic efforts such as tarpaulin or temporary roofing provide partial coverage. 4: Moderate Protection System:Structured protection is in place for most storage systems, reducing heat exposure. 5: Fully Protected Storage Systems:All water storage is located in shaded or insulated areas, fully protected from sunlight.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.7. Does the health care facility have water storage tanks with appropriate covers to protect from excessive heat?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.7. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Covers Installed:Water storage tanks lack any covers, making them vulnerable to contamination and excessive heat. 2: Minimal Covering Exists:Some tanks are covered, but materials or coverage may not effectively reduce heat or contamination. 3: Basic Covers Installed:Basic but incomplete coverage is provided to tanks; not all meet safety standards. 4: Moderate Coverage:Most tanks are properly covered and moderately insulated, with some variability in effectiveness. 5: Fully Covered and Heat-Protected:Tanks	All storage tanks are equipped with proper, heat-resistant covers in line with safety recommendations.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.8. Does the health care facility have chemicals stored away from excessive heat?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.8. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Storage Protocol:Chemicals are stored in hot environments without any measures to protect them from heat exposure. 2: Minimal Awareness of Risk:There is some awareness of proper storage, but implementation is limited or poorly enforced. 3: Basic Storage Measures:Some protective measures are in place, but not all chemical storage areas meet safety standards. 4: Moderate Compliance Achieved:Most chemical storage areas are kept away from heat, though occasional lapses may occur. 5: Fully Compliant Storage Practices	All chemicals are securely stored in cool, protected areas as per health and safety regulations:

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.9. Does the health care facility have health care waste stored away from excessive heat in cool and covered spaces?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-car-2.2.9. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Proper Storage Available:Health care waste is exposed to heat or sunlight without appropriate covering or cooling. 2: Minimal Control Measures:Some effort is made to move waste to shaded areas, but storage remains largely vulnerable to heat. 3: Basic Storage Facilities:Covered storage is provided but may be inconsistently used or insufficient for all waste types 4: Moderate Storage Capacity:Waste is generally stored in appropriate conditions, though cooling is not consistently maintained. 5: Fully Secured and Cool Storage:All healthcare waste is stored in cool, covered, and well-ventilated spaces in line with safety standards.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.1. Does the health care facility, work with water utility agencies to prevent suspension of services?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.1. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Engagement with Utility Agencies:The facility has no contact or coordination with water utility agencies and is unaware of potential service suspension risks. 2: Minimal enagement:The facility has basic contact with utility agencies but lacks formal coordination or collaboration to prevent service interruptions. 3: Basic enagement:Some communication protocols exist with utility agencies, including updates on potential service issues, but no joint planning. 4: Moderate enagement:The facility collaborates with utility agencies to identify and manage potential service disruptions, with informal agreements. 5: Full engageent:there is an active, ongoing partnership with water utility agencies including formal agreements and coordinated action plans.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.2. Does the health care facility have an alternative source of water supply?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.2. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Alternative Source:The facility relies solely on the main water supply and has no backup or alternative water source. 2: Minimal Alternative:A small quantity of stored water exists, which would last only a few hours or a day in case of supply interruption. 3: Basic Alternative Source:An alternative source (e.g., borehole or rainwater collection) is known but not yet fully operational or tested. 4: Moderate alternative Source:The alternative water source is partially functional and occasionally used, but not yet integrated into routine operations. 5: Fully Functional alternative source:A reliable and tested alternative water source is available, used during emergencies, and integrated into the water supply plan.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.3. Does the health care facility have a water safety plan in place, in case of water contamination?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.3. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Safety Plan:The facility has no documented plan or strategy to respond to water contamination events. 2: Minimal Plan:Staff are aware of contamination risks but response procedures are ad hoc and undocumented. 3: Basic  Plan:A draft water safety plan exists but lacks approval or implementation. 4: Moderate Plan Implemented:A water safety plan has been developed and partially implemented, with periodic reviews. 5: Fully Operational Plan:A comprehensive, regularly updated water safety plan is in place and guides all responses to contamination risks.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.4. Does the health care facility have a mechanism or regulation to carry out sanitary inspections of water supply, and when necessary, establish a temporary ban on use, until improvements are made?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.4. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Mechanism for Inspection:The facility has no policy or capacity to inspect water quality or restrict use in case of contamination. 2: Minimal mechanism:There is informal or occasional inspection without structured guidelines or authority to restrict water use. 3: Basic mechanism:An internal mechanism or draft protocol for sanitary inspection exists, but lacks enforcement power. 4: Moderate Mechanism:Sanitary inspections are conducted periodically with the ability to recommend temporary water restrictions. 5: Fully Functional mechanism:A clear and enforced regulation exists for sanitary inspections, including authority to restrict water use when needed.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.5. Does the health care facility have a contingency plan to ensure effective and timely delivery of safe water during floods and emergencies over the short- and long-term?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.5. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Contingency Plan:The facility has no plan for water delivery during emergencies or extreme weather. 2: Minimal Plan:Ad hoc measures may be applied during water shortage, but there is no documented plan or designated team. 3: Basic Plan Developed:A draft contingency plan exists, identifying basic logistics and actions during extreme weather events. 4: Moderate Plan:A contingency plan is in place and used during previous emergencies, but not comprehensive or reviewed often. 5: Fully Prepared and Reviewed Plan:A comprehensive plan exists for short- and long-term emergencies, regularly reviewed and tested for efficiency.

dataElements.name: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.6. Does the health care facility have a cross-sectoral water management plan to conserve and protect local or alternative water sources?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-wash-hsr-2.3.6. Does the health care faci
dataElements.description: 1: No Water Management Plan:The facility does not have any plan to conserve water or protect water sources. 2: Minimal Consideration:Some discussions have occurred with external stakeholders, but no structured collaboration or written plan exists. 3: Basic Plan in Draft:A water management plan is drafted and includes basic actions involving some external partners. 4: Moderate Plan in Operation:A cross-sectoral plan is implemented involving external agencies, though full integration is pending. 5: Fully Developed and Coordinated Plan:A fully integrated water management plan is in place with regular coordination with stakeholders to conserve and protect water sources.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.1 Does the health care facility regularly assess its energy system to ensure it can cope with heatwave conditions?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.1 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No assessment:The facility does not assess its energy system's ability to cope with heatwaves. 2: Minimal Awareness:Awareness of risks exists but no assessments have been conducted. 3: Basic Assessment:Occasional assessments take place, but not consistently or systematically. 4: Moderate Assessment:Regular assessments are conducted, but results are not fully integrated into operational plans. 5: Full/Comprehensive Assessment:Regular, documented assessments are carried out with clear mitigation actions to cope with heatwaves.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.2 Does the health care facility have an emergency backup generator (including fuel, where relevant) that is able to cover at least all critical service areas and equipment during heatwave events?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.2 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No emergency backup:There is no backup generator available, or it does not cover critical service areas. 2: Minimal emergency backup:There is minimal emergency backup generator capacity; covers only a few critical areas. 3: Basic emergency backup:An emergency backup generator is in place but covers only some critical areas and equipment. 4: Moderate emergency backup:Emergency backup generator covers most critical service areas, but not all. 5: Fully functional emergency backup:A fully functional emergency backup generator covers all critical service areas and equipment.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.3 Does the health care facility periodically check the emergency backup generator (including fuel, where relevant), even if rarely used?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.3 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No checks:There is no checks performed on emergency backup generators. 2: Minimal Checks:Emergency backup generators are checked irregularly, with no formal process. 3: Basic Checks:Emergency backup generators are checked periodically but without consistency or thoroughness. 4: Moderate checks:Emergency backup generators are checked regularly, but the process may need further refinement. 5: Full checks:Emergency backup generators are checked thoroughly and consistently as part of a formal procedure.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.4 Does the health care facility assess regularly heating, ventilation, and air conditioning systems?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.4 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No assessment:The facility does not assess its heating, ventilation, and air conditioning systems. 2: Minimal assessment:Informal checks occur but not as part of a formal system. 3: Basic assessments:Occasional assessments are performed, mostly reactive. 4: Moderate assessment:heating, ventilation, and air conditioning systems are reviewed periodically with basic maintenance schedules. 5: Full Assessment:heating, ventilation, and air conditioning systems are routinely assessed, maintained, and upgraded as needed.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.5 Does the health care facility assess whether renewable energy (if available, such as solar) is sufficient to power critical equipment?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.5 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Assessment of Renewable Energy:Renewable energy is available but never evaluated for emergency capacity. 2: Minimal Assessment:There is limited awareness but no formal assessment. 3: Basic Assessment:An initial assessment has been done with limited implementation. 4: Moderate Assessment:The facility periodically assesses capacity and integrates findings into plans. 5: Full assessments:Regular assessments are conducted to ensure renewable sources can support critical needs..

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.6 Does the health care facility monitor building humidity and if needed adjust the cooling system to control the humidity in operating room areas?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.1.6 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No  Monitoring:There is no system in place to monitor or control humidity. 2: Minimal Monitoring:Occasional or manual checks occur, without system support. 3: Basic Monitoring:A monitoring system exists but adjustments are manual or infrequent. 4: Moderate Monitoring:Humidity is monitored with basic controls available for adjustments. 5: Full monitoring:Automated systems monitor and adjust humidity to meet clinical standards.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-rm-3.2.1. Does the health care facility have a secure place to protect the backup generator (including fuel or battery storage, where relevant) from damage?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-rm-3.2.1. Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No protection:Generator and fuel are stored in exposed or vulnerable areas. 2: Minimal protection:A basic structure provides some protection but is not secure. 3: Basic protection:The system is stored in a lockable space but may be exposed to environmental damage. 4: Moderate protection:Storage is secure, covered, and partially weather-resistant. 5: Full protection:The generator and fuel are housed in a fully secured, climate-proof structure.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-rm-3.2.2. Does the health care facility have appliance thermometers in the refrigerator and freezer to determine if food, vaccines and other essential refrigeration-dependent medical supplies are safe?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-rm-3.2.2. Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Thermometers Present:The facility does not use appliance thermometers for cold storage. 2: Minimal Monitoring:One or two basic thermometers are used inconsistently. 3: Basic Monitoring:Thermometers exist but not in all storage units. 4: Moderate Monitoring:Most cold storage units have thermometers, with occasional monitoring. 5: Full Monitoring:All units are equipped with thermometers, and data is regularly recorded and reviewed.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-rm-3.2.3. Does the health care facility have adequate daylight to ensure proper visibility during power outages?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-rm-3.2.3. Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Daylight Access:The facility has poor access to natural light in critical areas. 2: Minimal Daylight:A few areas benefit from daylight, but most rely on artificial lighting. 3: Basic Daylight:Major service areas have access to daylight, but corridors or storage areas do not. 4: Moderate Daylight:Facility design incorporates daylight in most working areas. 5: Full Daylight:strategic facility design ensures daylight in all essential service areas for visibility.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-rm-3.2.4. Does the health care facility have power-operated doors that can be opened manually to permit exit in case of power failure?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-rm-3.2.4. Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Manual Option for Door:Power-operated doors cannot be used during outages. 2: Minimal Manual option:Some doors may be forced open manually, but this is difficult or unsafe. 3: Basic Manual Options:Select doors can be opened manually with effort. 4: Moderate Manual option:Most power doors have manual override features accessible to staff. 5: Full  Manual options:all power-operated doors have tested manual opening options for emergencies.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-rm-3.2.5. Does the health care facility have a clear guidance on heat-risk management for the maintenance of critical infrastructure (air conditioning, medical devices, computers, diagnostic equipment, boiling water
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-rm-3.2.5. Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No guidance:There is no written or verbal instruction on managing infrastructure during heat risks. 2: Minimal guidance:Staff follow informal instructions passed down verbally. 3: Basic guidance:General guidelines exist but do not cover all infrastructure components. 4: Moderate guidance:Written protocols exist for some key infrastructure during heatwaves. 5: Full guidance:Detailed, reviewed, and practised guidance is available for all key infrastructure.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.1.Does the health care facility have an emergency plan for power outages in the short- and long-term (during and after the event)?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.1.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:The healthcare facility has no emergency preparedness plan for power outages. 2: Minimal Plan:Initial conversations on developing a power outage emergency plan have occurred, but no formal document or action has been taken. 3: Basic Plan:A draft emergency plan exists but hasn't been implemented or reviewed for effectiveness. 4: Moderate Plan:A power outage emergency plan is in place and has been used, but it hasn't been recently revised or updated. 5: Full Plan:The healthcare facility has a comprehensive and up-to-date emergency plan for power outages that covers short- and long-term needs. The plan is routinely reviewed and tested.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.2.Does the health care facility have a plan or regulation to determine ways to reduce overall energy use?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.2.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:There is no consideration or documented approach to reducing energy consumption. 2: Minimal Plan:Preliminary discussions on energy reduction have occurred, but no official regulation or strategy is in place. 3: Basic Plan:A draft strategy for energy use reduction exists but has not been formalised or implemented. 4: Moderate Plan:An energy efficiency plan is in place but lacks recent updates or comprehensive implementation. 5: Full Plan:A current and actively implemented plan exists with clear guidelines and measurable targets to reduce energy usage.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.3.Does the health care facility work with energy utility agencies to prevent suspension of electricity services?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.3.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:No collaboration exists with utility providers to ensure continuity of electricity supply. 2: Minimal Plan:Early engagement with energy providers has taken place, but no formal agreements or strategies are in place. 3: Basic Plan:A draft partnership strategy exists with energy utilities, but it is not fully enacted. 4: Moderate Plan:Some agreements or plans are in place but haven't been reviewed or strengthened recently. 5: Full Plan:The facility actively collaborates with energy utilities under formal agreements or protocols to ensure uninterrupted power supply.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.4. Does the health care facility have a management plan for intermittent energy supplies or system failure?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.4. Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:There is no plan for managing energy supply interruptions or system failures. 2: Minimal Plan:Discussions on developing a management strategy for intermittent power supply have started but lack documentation. 3: Basic Plan:A draft energy supply management plan exists but has not yet been tested or finalised. 4: Moderate Plan:An energy failure management plan is operational but outdated or partially implemented. 5: Full plan:A robust and current plan exists to manage energy system failures, including mitigation and rapid response measures.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.5. Does the health care facility have an emergency plan to ensure availability of adequate lighting, communication and information systems, and refrigeration and sterilization equipment during the event?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.5. Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:The facility has no emergency plan for maintaining critical systems during power disruptions. 2: Minimal Plan with Initial Discussions:Some awareness and dialogue exist, but no formal planning or procedures have been developed. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:A draft plan has been created to address critical infrastructure needs during outages but hasn't been implemented. 4: Moderate Plan:An existing emergency plan partially addresses essential systems but is outdated. 5: Full Plan:A comprehensive and current emergency plan ensures continuous operation of lighting, communication, and essential equipment during outages.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.6.Does the health care facility have a plan to evacuate patients to a cooling station if the facility has lost power and has no other source of energy?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.6.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:There is no evacuation plan related to extreme heat and energy loss. 2: Minimal Plan with Initial Discussions:Discussions are ongoing, but no evacuation procedures or cooling station arrangements are in place. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:A draft evacuation and heat safety plan exists but is not tested or approved. 4: Moderate Plan in Place, Not Updated:An evacuation plan exists but lacks updates or adequate coordination with external facilities. 5: Fully Updated and Operational Plan:A well-maintained evacuation plan exists, including arrangements for safe patient transfer to cooling stations during total power loss.

dataElements.name: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.7. Does the health care facility ensure that walls and roofs are insulated?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-eng-hsr-3.3.7. Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:No insulation efforts or assessments have been conducted. 2: Minimal Plan with Initial Discussions:The facility has identified insulation needs but has not yet taken action. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:Plans or blueprints for insulation improvements exist but are pending execution. 4: Moderate Plan in Place, Not Updated:Some insulation measures have been applied, but the plan has not been reviewed or expanded recently. 5: Fully Updated and Operational Plan:The facility has implemented full insulation of walls and roofs and regularly assesses their condition for optimal temperature control.

dataElements.name:
heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.1 Does the health care facility  Provide greater advocacy on health workforce education to cover heatwave risks and 
 responses?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.1 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Advocacy Provided:No advocacy efforts are in place to promote education on heatwave risks and responses for the health workforce. The topic is not recognized or addressed in any capacity. 2: Minimal Advocacy Undertaken:Limited advocacy is undertaken. Heatwave-related health education may be mentioned in broader communications, but there are no dedicated efforts or consistent messaging. 3: Basic Advocacy Undertaken:Some structured advocacy initiatives exist. Heatwave risks and responses are occasionally promoted through campaigns or meetings, but coverage and reach remain limited. 4: Moderate Advocacy Undertaken:Regular and targeted advocacy efforts are made to promote education on heatwave-related health risks. Efforts reach a wide audience within the health workforce and are integrated into broader training or awareness initiatives. 5: Full/Comprehensive Knowledge, Experience, and Resources:Comprehensive and continuous advocacy is in place, with strategic efforts embedded in health workforce education programs. Messaging is evidence-based, regularly updated, and actively promoted through multiple platforms, including leadership engagement, policy briefs, training programs, and stakeholder collaboration.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.2 Does the health care facility Have preparedness and training for periods of extreme heat?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.2 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Preparedeness/Training:The facility has no training programs or preparedness measures in place for periods of extreme heat. Staff are unaware of associated health risks and response actions. 2: Minimal Preparedness/Training:Basic awareness exists among a few staff members, but there is no structured training or preparedness plan. Responses are ad hoc and not guided by formal protocols. 3: Basic Preparedness/Training:Some staff have received general training on extreme heat, and a basic preparedness plan exists. However, coverage is limited, and implementation is inconsistent across the facility. 4: Moderately Prepared:A clear preparedness plan is in place, and most staff have received training on how to respond during periods of extreme heat. Training is fairly routine and aligned with standard operating procedures. 5: Fully Prepared:Preparedness for extreme heat is institutionalized with a robust, regularly updated plan. All staff are comprehensively trained, with refresher sessions conducted periodically. Training includes simulations, role-specific responsibilities, and coordination with external emergency systems.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.3 Does the health care facility Assess the performance and vulnerabilities of each critical part of the facility (structural and nonstructural elements) that can be affected by hot temperatures?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.3 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Assessment Conducted:No assessment of facility components (structural or nonstructural) has been conducted in relation to heat impacts. Vulnerabilities remain unknown. 2: Minimal Assessment Conducted:Minimal collaboration with the local government occurs, but there is little or no focus on actively involving vulnerable populations in risk reduction management, policy making, or planning. Efforts are limited or ad-hoc. 3: Basic Assessment Conducted:A basic assessment has been conducted, covering some critical facility components (e.g., cooling systems or ventilation). However, it lacks comprehensive scope or depth, and some key vulnerabilities remain unassessed. 4: Moderate Assessment Conducted:A structured and fairly detailed assessment has been conducted or is actively underway. Both structural (e.g., roofing, insulation) and nonstructural (e.g., electrical systems, water supply) components are evaluated for heat-related performance issues. Results are used for planning but may need refinement or periodic updating. 5: Full Assessment Conducted Regularly:The facility regularly conducts comprehensive assessments of all critical components—both structural and nonstructural—against the risks posed by high temperatures. Assessments are data-driven, updated periodically, and fully integrated into facility management, maintenance, and emergency preparedness planning.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.4 Does the health care facility Assess the heating, ventilation and air-conditioning systems for capacity to deal with increasing heat and humidity?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.4 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Assessment conducted:No assessment has been done on HVAC systems for their capacity to manage rising heat and humidity. System performance under extreme conditions is unknown. 2: Minimal Assessment Conducted:Basic, informal checks or maintenance have been carried out (e.g., cleaning filters, visual inspection), but there is no structured evaluation of HVAC capacity under increasing climate stressors. 3: Basic Assessment Conducted:Some components of the HVAC systems have been assessed (e.g., cooling units), but the assessment does not fully address system-wide capacity, humidity control, or future climate projections. 4: Moderate Assessment Conducted:A detailed evaluation of HVAC systems has been conducted, including capacity under heatwave conditions and humidity control. Findings inform upgrades or improvements, though ongoing review mechanisms may be limited. 5: Full Assessment Conducted:A comprehensive and proactive assessment approach is in place. HVAC systems are regularly evaluated for current and projected climate conditions. Assessments are linked to maintenance schedules, facility upgrades, and contingency planning, ensuring resilience to increasing heat and humidity.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.5 Does the health care facility Install reflective white roofs to reduce heat impacts?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.5 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Awareness about Reflective Roof:There is no awareness or discussion of reflective roofing as a heat mitigation strategy. It is not included in facility plans or policies. 2: Minimal Awareness about Reflective Roof:Reflective roofing has been discussed or proposed but not yet implemented. It may be noted in future plans without a timeline or funding. 3: Basic Installation of Relective Roof:Reflective white roofs have been installed on some sections of the facility or in pilot areas, but full-scale implementation is pending due to resource or logistical constraints. 4: Moderate Installation of Relective Roof:Reflective roofing has been installed across most of the facility, with clear evidence of planning and follow-through. Only minor sections may still require implementation. 5: Full Installation of Reflective Roof:Reflective white roofs are fully installed across the facility as part of a broader heat-reduction strategy. Regular maintenance is conducted to ensure optimal performance, and the approach is integrated into long-term infrastructure planning.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.6 Does the health care facility Install green roofs to mitigate heat impacts?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.6 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Consideration Green Roof Installation:Green roofs have not been considered or discussed as a strategy for heat mitigation. There is no awareness or inclusion in facility plans or policies. 2: Minimal Consideration:Green roofs have been introduced as a concept or proposed in discussions or planning documents, but no practical steps have been taken toward implementation. 3: Basic Consideration:Green roofs have been implemented in a limited area (e.g., as a pilot project or on one building section), with some evaluation of their effectiveness underway. 4: Moderate consideration:Green roofs have been installed on multiple sections of the facility and are integrated into heat mitigation and sustainability strategies, though some areas may still be pending. 5: Full consideration:Green roofs are fully installed across all suitable areas of the facility and are actively maintained. Their impact is monitored, and they are part of a comprehensive, institutionalized climate adaptation and environmental sustainability plan.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.7 Does the health care facility Have pavements and roofs designed to withstand extreme temperatures or solar radiation?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.7 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Consideration for Heat Resistance:Pavements and roofs are constructed with standard materials without consideration for high temperatures or solar radiation. No protective measures are in place. 2: Minimal Consideration for Heat Resistance There is awareness of the need for heat-resistant materials, and discussions or plans may exist, but no upgrades or installations have been made yet: 3: Basic Consideration for Heat Resistance:Some areas of the facility have pavements and/or roofing materials adapted to withstand extreme heat (e.g., heat-resistant surfaces, insulating materials), but the approach is not yet comprehensive. 4: Moderately Consideration for Heat Resistance:Most pavements and roofs across the facility have been upgraded or constructed to withstand high temperatures and solar radiation. These measures are integrated into new construction and renovation plans. 5: Full/Comprehensive Consideration for Heat Resistance:All pavements and roofs are designed or retrofitted with heat-resistant and reflective materials to withstand extreme temperatures and solar radiation. The design is part of an institutional policy for climate resilience, and regular maintenance ensures sustained effectiveness.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.8 Does the health care facility Have light coloured paving on parking areas and walkways around the facility?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.8 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Consideration for Light Coloured Paving:All parking areas and walkways are constructed with dark or heat-absorbing materials. There is no consideration of reflective or light-coloured paving to mitigate heat. 2: Minimal Consideration for Light Coloured Paving:There is awareness of the benefits of light-coloured paving, and the concept has been introduced in planning or renovation discussions, but no implementation has occurred yet. 3: Basic Consideration for Light Coloured Paving:Light-coloured or reflective paving has been installed in select areas (e.g., entrances or specific walkways), but the majority of parking and walkway surfaces remain untreated. 4: Moderate Consideration:Most parking areas and walkways have been upgraded with light-coloured paving materials, and the facility has integrated this approach into its broader heat mitigation strategy. 5: Full Consideration All external surfaces, including parking areas and walkways, are constructed or retrofitted with light-coloured, heat-reflective materials:The practice is institutionalized and maintained as part of climate-smart infrastructure planning.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.9 Does the health care facility Review building code design baselines against extreme temperatures to ascertain inventory risks?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci- 4.1.9 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Review Conducted:Building design baselines have not been reviewed for climate resilience. There is no consideration of how extreme temperatures may pose risks to facility infrastructure or stored inventory. 2: Minimal Review Conducted:There is informal or ad hoc awareness of temperature-related risks, but no systematic review or documentation of building codes or design standards in relation to climate stressors. 3: Basic Review Conducted:A review of building code baselines has been initiated or partially completed, focusing on select areas (e.g., storage spaces, cold chain zones), but a comprehensive assessment is still lacking. 4: Moderately Review Conducted:A thorough review of facility building codes and design baselines has been conducted to assess risks to infrastructure and inventory due to extreme temperatures. Findings inform adaptation strategies. 5: Full/Comprehensive Review Conducted:Review of building code design baselines against extreme temperatures is a routine and institutionalized process. It is integrated into facility planning, inventory protection strategies, and climate adaptation policies, with regular updates based on climate data.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.10 Does the health care facility Identify vulnerabilities to estimate the possible loss and implemented actions to reduce impacts?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.10 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Assessment Conducted:There is no identification of vulnerabilities or estimation of potential losses due to climate-related or operational hazards. No actions have been planned or taken to reduce impacts. 2: Minimal Assessment:Basic visual inspection of structural and nonstructural elements to identify obvious signs of damage caused by flood exposure. 3: Basic Assessment:Some vulnerabilities have been identified, and preliminary estimates of potential loss have been made. A few targeted mitigation actions have been implemented, but efforts are not yet comprehensive or systematic. 4: Moderate Assessment Conducted:A thorough vulnerability assessment has been conducted, including estimations of potential losses. Clear, proactive measures have been implemented to reduce impacts, and these are periodically reviewed. 5: Full Assessment Coducted:Vulnerability and risk assessments are fully institutionalized, with regular updates based on new data. Loss estimations inform budgeting and planning. A comprehensive set of actions is in place and monitored to reduce and manage impacts effectively.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.11 Does the health care facility Have exterior shading devices, resilient trees or other architectural features that mitigate heat?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.11 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Heat Mitigating Features:The facility has no exterior shading, trees, or architectural features designed to reduce heat exposure. Outdoor areas are fully exposed to direct sunlight. 2: Minimal Awareness on Heat Mitigating Features:There is awareness of the benefits of shading and architectural heat-mitigation features. Some initial discussions or planning documents may include such elements, but nothing has been implemented yet. 3: Basic Heat Mitigating Features in Place:A few shading features (e.g. canopies, trees, pergolas) or heat-resilient design elements have been introduced in select areas, but the approach is not yet consistent or widespread across the facility. 4: Moderately Adequate Heat Mitigating Features in Place:Most areas of  the facility incorporate heat-mitigating features such as shaded walkways, heat-resilient trees, or design elements that reduce solar heat gain. These features are actively maintained and integrated into infrastructure plans. 5: Full/Comprehensive Heat Mitigating Features in Place:Exterior shading devices, green infrastructure (e.g. heat-tolerant trees), and architectural features that mitigate heat are comprehensively implemented and maintained across the facility. These measures are part of a strategic, climate-resilient design framework, regularly evaluated for effectiveness.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.12 Does the health care facility Have windows that can be operated to provide for ventilation and maintain habitable and operational conditions?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.12 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Operable Windows:Windows are fixed or sealed, offering no option for natural ventilation. Indoor conditions rely entirely on mechanical systems, which may be insufficient or non-functional during extreme heat or power outages. 2: Minimal Cooling System:Cooling is limited to basic methods such as handheld fans, open windows, or occasional use of portable fans in select areas. Effectiveness is minimal and not suitable for prolonged heat or large spaces. 3: Basic Operable Windows:There is a cooling system (e.g. ceiling fans, wall units, or limited air conditioning) available in some areas of the facility. However, it does not cover all essential areas or maintain consistent comfort during extreme heat. 4: Moderately Operable Windows:Most facility windows are operable and strategically placed to support natural airflow and thermal comfort. Ventilation is effective in maintaining operational and habitable conditions in most areas. 5: Fully Operable Windows:All necessary windows are operable and integrated into a well-designed natural ventilation system that complements mechanical systems. Cross-ventilation is maximized, and windows are maintained to ensure effective use during heatwaves or system failures.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.13 Does the health care facility Have a system for cooling the environment?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.13 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Cooling System to Support the Natural Sytem:There is no system in place to cool the indoor environment. The facility relies entirely on natural airflow or remains exposed to external heat without any form of temperature control. 2: Minimal Cooling System:Cooling is limited to basic methods such as handheld fans, open windows, or occasional use of portable fans in select areas. Effectiveness is minimal and not suitable for prolonged heat or large spaces. 3: Basic Cooling System:There is a cooling system (e.g. ceiling fans, wall units, or limited air conditioning) available in some areas of the facility. However, it does not cover all essential areas or maintain consistent comfort during extreme heat. 4: Moderate Cooling System:A functional, facility-wide cooling system (such as split AC units, central air, or industrial fans) is in place and capable of maintaining habitable conditions during heatwaves across most operational areas. 5: Full/Comprehensive Cooling System:An advanced, energy-efficient, and climate-responsive cooling system is fully integrated into facility infrastructure. It includes zoning, backup power, regular maintenance, and adjustments based on external temperatures to ensure optimal thermal comfort and resilience.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.14 Does the health care facility Provide an extra medical supply in case of increased demand for treatment of heat stress?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.14 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Preparedness:No extra medical supplies are available for treating heat stress. The facility relies entirely on regular stock, which may not be sufficient during a surge in demand. 2: Minimal Preparedness:There is awareness of the need, and some additional supplies (e.g. oral rehydration salts, IV fluids) may be available, but they are not part of a formal preparedness plan. Stock levels are inconsistent and may not meet actual demand. 3: Basic Preparedness:A small, dedicated reserve of essential heat stress treatment supplies is maintained. This includes some fluids, medications, and cooling aids, but quantities may be limited or not regularly reviewed. 4: Moderate Preparedness:The facility maintains a clearly defined stockpile of medical supplies specifically for heat-related conditions, with regular checks and restocking. Supplies are sufficient to handle a moderate surge in cases and are part of a broader emergency preparedness plan. 5: Full/Comprehensive Preparedness:A robust and well-managed medical supply system is in place, fully integrated with contingency planning for extreme heat events. Supplies are forecasted based on risk assessments, routinely updated, and linked to early warning systems for proactive mobilization.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.15 Does the health care facility Stimulate increase of water intake by staff and patients?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.15 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Efforts made at all:There are no measures or initiatives to encourage increased water intake. Staff and patients receive no guidance, prompts, or support related to hydration. 2: Minimal Efforts made:Occasional, informal reminders may be given by staff, but there are no structured messages, visual cues, or institutional practices promoting hydration, especially during heat events. 3: Basic Efforts made:Basic efforts such as posters, verbal reminders, or informal hydration talks are implemented during hot periods. However, these are not consistent or systematically reinforced across the facility. 4: Moderately Adequate Efforts made:Hydration messages and reminders are integrated into daily routines or care practices. Visual aids, PA announcements, or regular prompts are used to encourage both staff and patients to drink water regularly, especially during high-temperature periods. 5: Full/Comprehensively Adequate Efforts made:A proactive hydration strategy is in place, tailored for both staff and patients. It includes routine reminders, easily accessible drinking water stations, staff training, and response protocols during heatwaves. Hydration promotion is institutionalized and monitored as part of occupational and patient health practices.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.16 Does the health care facility Have insulated loft and cavity walls?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.16 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Insulation:The facility has no insulation in the loft or cavity walls. Heat easily penetrates indoor spaces, significantly impacting thermal comfort and energy efficiency. 2: Minimal Insulation:Some insulation exists but is outdated, poorly installed, or only covers either the loft or a limited section of the cavity walls.
