Its impact on temperature regulation is minimal. 3: Basic Insulation:Both loft and cavity walls are insulated with standard materials. The insulation is functional but may not meet current standards for heat resistance or may not be optimized for local climate conditions. 4: Moderately Adequate insulation:The facility is equipped with quality insulation in both loft and cavity walls. It is properly installed, well-maintained, and contributes significantly to reducing heat transfer and improving indoor comfort. 5: Full/Comprehensively Adequate insulation:The facility has high-grade, energy-efficient insulation throughout the loft and cavity walls, designed specifically for extreme heat. The system is regularly inspected and upgraded as needed, ensuring optimal performance and contributing to overall climate resilience.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.17 Does the health care facility Have a plan for arranging for extra staffing for emergency support services?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.17 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:The facility has no documented or informal plan to arrange extra staffing during emergencies. Staffing remains fixed regardless of emergency needs or surges in demand. 2: Minimal Plan:There is general awareness of the need for extra staffing during emergencies, but arrangements are reactive, informal, and not based on a structured or written plan. 3: Basic Plan:A basic contingency staffing plan exists. It identifies key support roles and suggests procedures for mobilizing additional staff during emergencies, though it may lack clarity on timelines, roles, or approval processes. 4: Moderately Plan:A well-defined plan is in place to arrange for extra staffing, including defined roles, contact lists, deployment timelines, and coordination mechanisms. The plan has been communicated to relevant teams and may have been tested in drills or past events. 5: Fully/Comprehensive Plan:A comprehensive emergency staffing strategy is in place, integrated into the facility’s emergency preparedness framework. It includes pre-identified surge staff, agreements with external support (e.g., health agencies or volunteer networks), routine simulations, and mechanisms for rapid activation, ensuring operational continuity during emergencies.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.18 Does the health care facility Store chemicals away from excessive heat?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.18 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No Precautions Taken:Chemicals are stored without regard for temperature conditions. Storage areas are exposed to heat sources or direct sunlight, posing serious safety and degradation risks. 2: Minimal Precautions Taken:There is some awareness of the need to protect chemicals from heat, but storage practices are inconsistent. Some chemicals may be shielded, but no formal system or guidelines are in place. 3: Basic Precautions Taken:Chemicals are generally stored in shaded or relatively cool areas. Basic precautions are taken to avoid exposure to heat, but the storage area may still be vulnerable during extreme temperatures or lacks active monitoring. 4: Moderate Precautions Taken:Chemicals are stored in well-ventilated, designated areas away from heat sources. Storage guidelines are followed consistently, and temperature conditions are routinely monitored to prevent exposure to excessive heat. 5: Full/Comprehensive Precautions Taken:A dedicated chemical storage system with temperature control and real-time monitoring is in place. The facility adheres strictly to chemical safety regulations and conducts regular audits to ensure compliance, minimizing all heat-related risks.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.19 Does the health care facility Have a monitoring and early warning system integrated with other areas to manage risks related to heatwave impacts on the facility?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.19 Does the health care
dataElements.description: 1: No System in place:There is no monitoring or early warning system for heatwaves. The facility relies entirely on external alerts (if any), with no internal preparedness or response coordination. 2: Minimal System in place:The facility informally monitors weather updates (e.g., through news or mobile apps), but there is no structured system or integration with other operational areas. Responses to heatwaves are reactive and not systematically managed. 3: Basic System in place:A basic early warning mechanism is in place to monitor heatwave risks (e.g., through meteorological alerts), but it operates independently and is not fully integrated with health services, logistics, or emergency planning within the facility. 4: Moderate System in place:The facility has a structured monitoring and early warning system for heatwaves that is integrated with key departments (e.g., emergency response, health services, and facility operations). It supports proactive measures like staffing adjustments, hydration protocols, and heat-related patient care. 5: Full/Comprehensive System in place:A robust, real-time monitoring and early warning system is fully integrated with all relevant facility operations (e.g., patient care, supply chain, HR, infrastructure management). It enables predictive planning, automated alerts, cross-sector coordination, and rapid mobilization of resources during heatwave events.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.20 v Have an effective emergency risk communication plan to communicate clear messages of the danger of heatwaves, emphasizing health protection as a priority?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-asci-4.1.20 v Have an effective
dataElements.description: 1: No Emergency Communication Plan:There is no formal or informal plan in place to communicate heatwave-related risks. Staff and patients are not informed about heatwave dangers or protective measures. 2: Minimal plan:Some effort is made to share information on heatwave dangers, but communication is inconsistent, not targeted, and not based on a structured plan. Messaging may be unclear or reach only a limited audience. 3: Basic plan:A basic risk communication plan exists for heatwaves, including standard messages on heat risks and protection. Communication channels (e.g., notice boards, internal memos, or public announcements) are identified but may not be consistently used or evaluated. 4: Moderate plan:The facility has a well-developed emergency risk communication plan that includes clear, actionable messages about heatwave dangers. Messages are adapted to different audiences (e.g., staff, patients, community members), using appropriate and accessible channels. The plan is reviewed periodically for effectiveness. 5: Full/Comprehensive plan:A comprehensive, evidence-based risk communication plan is in place, with proactive dissemination of targeted, culturally appropriate heatwave messages. It is integrated into broader emergency preparedness efforts, includes feedback mechanisms, multilingual outreach (if applicable), and uses multiple channels (SMS, radio, posters, digital). The system is regularly tested, updated, and responsive to changing conditions.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.1 Does the health care facility Receive meteorological information on the likelihood of forthcoming hot weather?*
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.1 Does the health care f
dataElements.description: 1: No Access to Metreological Information:The facility does not receive or access any weather forecasts or meteorological information related to heat events. 2: Minimal Access to Metreological Information:Meteorological information is occasionally accessed through informal channels (e.g., TV, radio, personal mobile apps), but there is no routine or organized approach to monitoring heat forecasts. 3: Basic Access to Metreological Information The facility regularly monitors general meteorological updates (e:g., national weather services, local forecasts), but there is no dedicated or customized alert system for the facility. 4: Moderately Access to Metreological Information:The facility has a system in place to receive timely and reliable meteorological forecasts specific to heat events. Information is integrated into operational planning and early action protocols. 5: Full/Comprehensive Access to Metreological Information:The facility is connected to a formal meteorological early warning system that provides real-time, location-specific forecasts and alerts. Alerts are automated and linked to internal response mechanisms (e.g., activating heat contingency plans, notifying staff).

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.2 Does the health care facility Have a syndromic surveillance system for heat-related illnesses?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.2 Does the health care f
dataElements.description: 1: No Surveillance System in place  No surveillance system in place and no plans developed: 2: Minimal Surveillance System in place  Informal tracking or ad hoc data collection during extreme heat events: 3: Basic Surveillance System in place:A basic system is in place in select departments/facilities, but not standardized. 4: Moderate Surveillance System in place:Systematic syndromic surveillance is active in most health facilities. 5: Fully Functional Surveillance System in place:Fully functional and integrated syndromic surveillance system with regular updates and inter-agency data sharing.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.3 Does the health care facility Have an updated training programme for the health workforce to detect and track climate change-related human heat stress?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.3 Does the health care f
dataElements.description: 1: No Training Material:No training or outdated materials in use. 2: Minimal Training Material:Occasional workshops or references to heat stress in unrelated trainings. 3: Basic Training Material:Structured training exists but is not regularly updated or widely disseminated. 4: Moderate Training Prgram:Ongoing training programs exist, integrated into professional development schedules. 5: Full/comprehensive Training Program:Comprehensive, routinely updated curriculum in place and accessible across all health levels.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.4 Does the health care facility Have a long-term strategy for reducing heat, such as through building insulation?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.4 Does the health care f
dataElements.description: 1: No Strategy for reducing heat in place:No strategy or policy exists for heat reduction. 2: Minimal Strategy in place Awareness exists, but actions are limited or reactive: 3: Basic Strategy in place:Strategy is drafted or piloted in limited facilities. 4: Moderately Adequate Strategy in place:Strategy implemented in key infrastructure with measurable targets. 5: Full/Comprehensive Strategy in place:Fully operational, regularly reviewed, and integrated into infrastructure and climate resilience planning.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.5 Does the health care facility Perform risk assessments to assist with adaptation measures for heatwaves?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.5 Does the health care f
dataElements.description: 1: No Risk Assessment conducted:No risk assessments conducted. 2: Minimal Risk Assessment conducted:Preliminary assessments or outdated data used. 3: Basic Assessment:Periodic assessments conducted, but not consistently applied in planning 4: Moderate Assessment:Regular and structured risk assessments inform response strategies. 5: Full/Comprehensive Assessment:Risk assessments are institutionalized, feeding into national and local climate adaptation planning.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.6 Does the health care facility Have an information system for tracking and monitoring diseases following heatwave events?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.6 Does the health care f
dataElements.description: 1: No Infromation System:No system in place to track post-heatwave disease incidence. 2: Minimal infromation System:Some manual records kept, but not centralized or shared. 3: Basic infromation System:A basic information system exists with limited functionality. 4: Moderate Infromation System:Digital system in place for data collection and early warnings. 5: Full infromation System:Comprehensive system with real-time analytics, integrated with health surveillance and meteorological alerts.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.7 Does the health care facility Have measures that improve health performance, based on a history of climate variability in the region or locality?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.7 Does the health care f
dataElements.description: 1: No Consideration of past climate patterns:No consideration of past climate patterns in health performance planning. 2: Minimal Consideration:Limited awareness of historical climate variability, with scattered or informal efforts. 3: Basic Consideration:Some measures implemented in response to past climate events, but not systematized. 4: Moderate Consideration:Health performance strategies are informed by documented local climate history. 5: Full Consideration:Comprehensive, evidence-based health performance measures aligned with long-term climate data and regularly reviewed for effectiveness.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.8 Does the health care facility Perform evaluations to predict heatwave conditions 1–5 days in advance?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.8 Does the health care f
dataElements.description: 1: No Evaluation performed:No evaluation or forecasting capacity for heatwave conditions. 2: Minimal Evaluation performed:Reliance on general weather reports without tailored heatwave evaluations. 3: Basic Evaluation performed:Basic forecasting tools in use, but not consistently updated or localized. 4: Moderately Adequate Evaluation performed:Evaluations are routinely conducted using meteorological data to forecast heatwaves. 5: Full/Comprehensive Evaluation performed:Advanced early warning systems provide reliable 1–5 day forecasts, directly linked to health preparedness protocols.

dataElements.name: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.9 Does the health care facility Coordinate public broadcasts of information about the anticipated timing, severity and duration of heatwave conditions in its surrounding communities?
dataElements.shortName: heatwave-infraTTP-pst-4.2.9 Does the health care f
dataElements.description: 1: No Mechanism for public communication:No mechanism for public communication on heatwave threats. 2: Minimal Mechniasm for public communication:Occasional or ad hoc broadcasts during extreme weather events. 3: Basic Mechanism for public communication:Some coordination exists, with irregular or delayed dissemination of heatwave information. 4: Moderate Mechanism for public communication:Structured communication strategy in place; timely information reaches most of the population. 5: Full Mechanism for public communication:Fully integrated broadcast system, using multiple platforms to reach all segments of the community with clear, timely, and actionable messages.

dataElements.description: 1: No Dedicated Budget:No dedicated budget for heatwave emergency preparedness. 2: Minimal Dedicated Budget:Occasional, ad hoc allocations during emergencies. 3: Basic Dedicated Budget:Occasional, ad hoc allocations during emergencies. 4: Moderate Dedicated Budget:Dedicated line item in annual budget; periodically reviewed and adjusted. 5: Full/Comprehensive Budget:Well-defined, sustainable budget fully integrated into core financial planning and aligned with long-term climate resilience goals.

dataElements.description: 1: No Governance Mechanism for:No formal governance mechanisms for climate or heatwave risk. 2: Minimal Governance Mechanism:Informal or reactive coordination; low stakeholder engagement. 3: Basic Governance Mechanism:Some institutional measures in place; limited multi-sectoral collaboration. 4: Moderate Governance Mechanism:Functional governance structures with clear responsibilities for evaluation and response. 5: Full/Comprehensive Governance Mechanism:Adaptive governance embedded in institutional processes; proactive, inclusive, and dynamic with strong community and inter-agency coordination.
